title: Generating quantum ensembles via reverse-time quantum diffusions
authors: Maël Bompais, Mădălin Guţă, Juan P. Garrahan
abstract: We establish a reverse-time denoising theory for quantum diffusions of continuously measured quantum systems. Starting from the stochastic Schrödinger equation of a forward noising dynamics, we derive the exact reverse-time dynamics for quantum trajectories, whose law coincides with the time-reversal of the original process. We prove that the denoising dynamics is a physically admissible quantum diffusion, with the same measurement-induced noise but a state-dependent feedback Hamiltonian, a direct analogue of the "score function" of generative classical diffusion models. This provides a principled framework for converting samples of a simple distribution into those of a more complex ensemble of quantum states. We show how the denoising dynamics can be directly learnt from forward trajectory data, and how to exploit purification to initialise the denoising process.
summary: This paper develops a rigorous theory for generating complex quantum state ensembles by implementing a reverse-time quantum diffusion process. It shows that the reverse dynamics is a controlled quantum diffusion whose Hamiltonian acts as a quantum score function. This provides a general, principled method for quantum state preparation from simple noise distributions.
main_problem: The paper establishes a principled framework to generate complex quantum state ensembles by reversing a quantum diffusion process, analogous to classical score-based generative models.
main_result: It derives the exact reverse-time dynamics, showing it is a feedback-controlled quantum diffusion governed by a state-dependent Hamiltonian that acts as the quantum analogue of the score function.
method: The derivation involves analyzing the reverse-time transition kernel using Bayes' formula and solving the corresponding backward Kolmogorov equation to find the required stochastic differential equation (SDE).
model_or_system: The system involves quantum states evolving via continuous measurement, modeled by a forward Stochastic Schrödinger Equation (SSE) leading to an invariant distribution. The reverse process is a controlled quantum diffusion.
key_observables: The Wasserstein distance $W_1(	ilde{\mu}_t, \mu_0)$ is used to confirm convergence to the target distribution $\mu_0$. The mean Frobenius norm of the reverse Hamiltonian $\mathbb{E}[\|	ilde{H}_t(	ilde{\psi}_t)\|^2]$ is also monitored.
important_parameters: The time parameter $t$ (or $T-t$), the initial distribution $\mu_0$, and the invariant distribution $\mu_{	ext{inv}}$ are key. The Hamiltonian $	ilde{H}_t(\psi)$ depends explicitly on the instantaneous state $\psi$.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes suitable regularity conditions for the transition kernel and relies on the Markov property. For specific examples, rotational symmetry around an axis $\hat{n}$ is assumed.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the forward noising dynamics spreading samples from $\mu_0$ towards $\mu_{	ext{inv}}$ and the reverse-time dynamics reconstructing $\mu_0$ from $\mu_{	ext{inv}}$. Figure 2 shows the convergence of the Wasserstein distance and the Hamiltonian norm for a qubit example.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the general theory by deriving the reverse-time SDE from the forward process's transition kernel. It then proves necessary algebraic conditions for the reverse Hamiltonian and applies these results to specific, solvable models (like the qubit) to explicitly calculate the score function and the final Hamiltonian.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, general mathematical tool for quantum state preparation and tomography, linking modern machine learning generative models (diffusion models) directly to fundamental open quantum system dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Forward (noising) dynamics for the 2-level example for samples from an initial measure over pure states µ0(ψ) corresponding to a great circle in the Bloch sphere. Under the noising dynamics the samples evolve towards a uniform µinv = 1/4π. (b) Backward denoising dynamics starting from uniformly distributed samples, for various levels ℓmax of approximation of the score, see [13] for details. At long times the initial µ0(ψ) is reconstructed, thus transforming noisy initial pure states to samples of the distribution of interest.

FIG. 2: Norm of the average reverse Hamiltonian in Eq.(20) as a function of denoising time in the two-level example, for several variational cut-offs ℓmax. Inset: Wasserstein distance of the learned distribution of ˜ψ to the target distribution, as a function of denoising time.
